text:
Prompt of the Day: SCP ENCOUNTER FILE 📁⚠️💜💚

Today’s Prompt of the Day turns your character reference image into a cinematic SCP encounter file.

The final image is locked to a 21:9 ultra-wide layout:
the left quarter becomes an SCP-style document panel, while the right three-quarters becomes the full encounter scene.
The SCP is randomized by default, or you can replace the top field with a specific SCP number.

Have fun with this one — and maybe don’t blink. ⚠️

............................PROMPT STARTS HERE............................

Selected SCP:
[currently randomized]

Input rule:
Replace [currently randomized] with the SCP number only if you want a specific SCP.
Example: [173] or [3001]
Selection rule:
If the field still says [currently randomized], choose a real SCP at random from the public SCP Foundation database.
If the entered SCP number does not match a real SCP entry, use the closest real SCP number instead.
Use only real SCP entries.
Do not invent a fake SCP number, fake SCP title, or fake SCP lore.

Use the attached character reference image as the only character identity reference.
The attached reference image is the full identity source for the character design.
Character reference rules:
Preserve the attached character’s face shape, hairstyle, hair colour, eye colour, body language, signature colour palette, key outfit motifs, species traits, accessories, silhouette, and overall character vibe.

The final design must still clearly look like the attached character.
Do not redesign the attached character into a different person.
Hard style rule:
Preserve the attached reference image’s visual art style and character identity while placing the character into a direct encounter with the selected SCP.
If the reference is anime, keep it anime. If it is stylized, keep that stylization.
Do not turn the character photorealistic unless specifically requested.
Layout and format:
Create a 21:9 ultra-wide cinematic image.
The left-hand one-quarter of the image should be a clean SCP-style document panel.
The right-hand three-quarters of the image should be the full cinematic encounter scene.
The document panel and the encounter scene should feel like one unified finished image, but clearly separated in function.
Left panel — SCP document section:
Use the left 25% of the image as a readable SCP dossier or document-style information panel.
This panel should contain:

the selected SCP number
the SCP name or designation if known

the object class if known

a brief description of what the SCP is
a brief explanation of what it does, how it behaves, or what danger it poses
Keep the writing concise and readable.
Use a few short paragraph blocks rather than a giant wall of text.
The wording should feel like SCP file language, containment database language, or formal anomaly report language.
Visually, the panel should resemble a clean SCP document, classified case sheet, or Foundation report page.
Use a minimal, technical, readable SCP-style presentation.

Right panel — encounter scene:
Use the right 75% of the image for the actual scene only.
This section should contain the attached character reference and the selected SCP in a direct encounter.
Do not place SCP numbers, labels, descriptions, dossier text, or explanatory text inside the encounter scene itself.
Let this side focus entirely on the image, story, action, and atmosphere.
Core scene rule:
The selected SCP must control the scene.
Do not default to a generic SCP lab, containment chamber, or research hallway unless that specific SCP genuinely requires it.
Instead, build the encounter around what the selected SCP actually is, what it does, how it manifests, and what kind of danger, surreal effect, or altered reality it creates.
Scene concept:
Create a cinematic direct encounter between the attached character and the selected SCP.
The character should be actively experiencing the SCP, not merely standing beside it.
Show the character being stalked, hunted, attacked, watched, lured, trapped, corrupted, psychologically affected, reality-warped, or otherwise impacted in a way that matches the selected SCP’s real lore and behavior.
The scene should feel immediate, story-driven, and clearly shaped by the SCP’s actual nature.
Encounter logic:
If the selected SCP is a predator, hunter, or stalker, show the character being pursued, cornered, threatened, or attacked.
If the selected SCP is psychological, memetic, conceptual, or reality-bending, show the character visibly reacting to its effects and show the world around them altered by it.
If the selected SCP is subtle or indirect, emphasize its consequences, atmosphere, distortions, and impact rather than forcing a generic monster reveal.
If the selected SCP is best represented through an object, environment, event, or impossible phenomenon, portray it in the most lore-appropriate way.

Character role:
The attached character should remain themselves while being placed into the SCP encounter.
They may appear as a victim, survivor, witness, explorer, bystander, wanderer, investigator, or other lore-fitting role depending on the SCP.
Use the attached character’s colours, motifs, accessories, outfit shapes, and overall vibe as the foundation for how they appear in the scene.
The result should feel custom-built for that character, not generic.
Environment and composition:
Set the scene in the kind of place that naturally fits the selected SCP.
This may be an urban street, forest, tunnel, staircase, apartment, road, classroom, ruin, alternate reality, impossible structure, outdoor landscape, corrupted environment, or any other SCP-appropriate location.
Keep the right-side scene cinematic, readable, and visually strong.
Maintain clear hierarchy, with the character and the SCP-related focal point as the primary subjects.
Lighting and mood:
Let the selected SCP dictate the mood.
Use lighting, atmosphere, and visual tone that fit the SCP’s behavior and emotional impact.
Predatory SCPs should feel tense and dangerous.
Surreal or reality-warping SCPs should feel strange, unstable, and uncanny.
Tragic or psychologically heavy SCPs should feel haunting and atmospheric.
The encounter should feel specific to the chosen SCP, not like generic horror.
Quality and rendering:
Polished, premium-quality stylized illustration with clean linework, crisp rendering, strong composition, readable forms, cinematic atmosphere, and clear storytelling.
The final result should feel like a high-end SCP encounter image paired with an integrated Foundation dossier panel.
Do not:

Do not invent a fake SCP.
Do not ignore the Selected SCP field.
Do not use a non-existent SCP if a real closest match can be used.

Do not default to a generic lab scene unless the selected SCP genuinely requires it.
Do not make the encounter a simple portrait or static pose.
Do not place SCP description text inside the right-side encounter scene.
Do not make the left document panel unreadable, overly tiny, or excessively long.
Do not make the scene feel generic if the selected SCP has a specific identity, behavior, or atmosphere.

Do not change the attached character’s identity.
Do not add extra unrelated main characters.
Do not make the composition cluttered, muddy, or hard to read.
Do not use photorealism unless specifically requested.

..............................END OF PROMPT..................................

#POTD #promptoftheday #AI #AiArt #Art #AnimeArt #SCP #SCPFoundation #HorrorArt #Anomaly #CharacterDesign #DigitalArt #AnimeStyle #CommunityPrompt

I've spent a lot of hours using AI for work. In fact, i'd wager i have as many hours and tokens logged as _anyone_ using AI in a professional capacity where the outcome was critical to doing my job or achieving a specific business objective. Over the last year or so, it became very clear that there were 2 major systematic risks to the way i had grown reliant on working:
1) git and github - git is poorly suited to ai work. when you spin up multiple agents at the same time, branches and worktrees become stale, after dozens of changes and PRs, reviews diverge causing constant conflicts, repos become large and unwieldy and adrift in drity state. On top of that, cloning giant repos and setting up dev envs every time i wanted to spin up an agent became cumbersome. sure you can use docker to help but its still stale and required packaging and cloning step(s). neither git or github was designed for my new ai centric, dozens to hundreds of agents working on large repos / monorepos simultaneously, workflows. managing state and envs and conflicts and iops became a full time job.
2) claude code and anthropic - i had grown reliant on a model and a powerful harness and on that model being trained to be an expert in that harness. I needed to be able to fine tune a model for my use cases but also have it be a master of the tools and flows of a specific harness. vertically integrating and fine tuning the model on the harness and tasks you want to accomplish is one of the most important aspects to actually being productive with these systems.

When i took a step back to figure out how to aggressively de-risk but also how to actually be productive as a single dev managing tons of semi-supervised AI, i figured i needed to go back to the beginning.

Lets try to take an example from Google who has similar issues with scale and tooling and setup (albeit with human developers at least when i was there) . If you wanted to build a system that was based on their best practices to manage all those moving parts and thousands of developers working in large monorepos, how would you do it? It wouldn't just be a simple git forge, git itself is a huge part of the problem. if you were starting from scratch, you'd want to make sure you designed the system from the ground up for ai agents and workflows for the new world.

Well to start, you'd make shallow virtual checkouts and clones so you never had to pull the full repo to disk and your clones would be instant. then you'd use jj to keep track of your draft state with a real jj native persistence layer and backend. You'd use sapling to create commit stacks and you'd keep all of your workspaces automatically in sync with a cloud sync system. You'd optimize this system from the ground up to make ai agents and reviewers first class citizens , you'd reduce startup time to seconds and you'd have per file ACLs so you never had agents run wild.

And if you were going to do all of that, you'd also want to vertically integrate EVERYTHING from the model to coding harness to scm to tools to remote cloud runtimes and platforms to optimize end to end for this new world and its new workflows. 

So thats what i built and i've been using it exclusively to build and run our research lab and company for the last 6 months or so. This was not only a great way to pressure test the models, tools and the workflows but it was also an interesting research project on its own. Could a single developer use AI to be productive enough to launch a real, high quality product that would normally take a team of hundreds of engineers to build, launch and operate? I suppose you will have to be the judge of how successful it ultimately was but the project as of today is ~25 million lines of code and dozens of services and automation. in my opinion it has been an overwhelming success and i cant imagine going back to working any other way.

So, today I am starting the process of making the same tools i have been using internally for the last 6 months available to all of you. this will start with the code app that i released a few days ago and access to our inference engine to power that app. soon we will announce more tools and features including access to the ncode scm and ncode platform. there will be more formal announcements about these new platforms, tools and features over the coming days / weeks as we begin rolling out access. I hope y'all find them as useful as i have.  

https://t.co/bJmg9QPGUH

🧠 Entropy: One of the Most Important Concepts in AI, Machine Learning, Information Theory, and Physics

If you understand entropy, you'll better understand:

• Decision Trees
• Cross Entropy Loss
• LLM Training
• Compression Algorithms
• Diffusion Models
• Statistical Physics
• Information Theory

Entropy is everywhere.

---

What is Entropy?

Entropy measures uncertainty, randomness, or information content.

The more unpredictable something is, the higher its entropy.

The more predictable something is, the lower its entropy.

Think of entropy as:

"How surprised would I be by the next outcome?"

---

Example 1: Coin Toss

Fair Coin:

Heads = 50%
Tails = 50%

You cannot predict the outcome.
Maximum uncertainty.
High entropy.

---

Biased Coin:

Heads = 99%
Tails = 1%

You can almost always predict Heads.

Low uncertainty.
Low entropy.

---

Example 2: Language Models

Consider predicting the next word:

"The sun rises in the ____"

Possible next token:

• east → 95%
• west → 3%
• sky → 2%

Very predictable.

Low entropy.

---

Now:

"Today I dreamed about a ____"

Possible next token:

• dragon
• robot
• elephant
• spaceship
• castle

Many possibilities.

Higher uncertainty.
Higher entropy.

---

Claude Shannon's Insight

In 1948, Claude Shannon introduced information entropy.

The formula:

Entropy = Expected Information Content

A highly predictable event carries little information.
A surprising event carries a lot of information.

---

Why Compression Works

Suppose a file contains:
AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA

Very repetitive.

Low entropy.
Easy to compress.

---

Suppose a file contains:
X7$P@9L#2W!K8Z&Q

Looks random.

High entropy.
Difficult to compress.

Compression is essentially exploiting low entropy.

---

Entropy in Decision Trees

Algorithms like:

• ID3
• C4.5
• Random Forests

Use entropy to determine the best split.

Good splits:

Reduce uncertainty.

Bad splits:

Leave uncertainty unchanged.

The objective:

Maximum Information Gain.

---

Entropy in Deep Learning

Modern neural networks use:

Cross Entropy Loss

For:

• Classification
• Language Models
• Transformers
• LLMs

Cross entropy measures:

How different are model predictions from reality?

Lower cross entropy means better predictions.

---

Entropy in LLM Training

During pretraining:

Model
↓
Predict Next Token
↓
Compute Cross Entropy Loss
↓
Update Weights
↓
Reduce Prediction Error

Every GPT-style model optimizes cross entropy billions of times during training.

---

Entropy and Temperature

LLMs use temperature during generation.

Low Temperature:

• Lower entropy
• More deterministic
• More predictable

High Temperature:

• Higher entropy
• More creative
• More diverse

Temperature directly affects uncertainty in generated text.

---

Entropy in Reinforcement Learning

Modern RL algorithms often maximize entropy.

Why?

Without entropy:

Agent repeats the same actions.

With entropy:

Agent explores new possibilities.

Exploration requires uncertainty.

---

Entropy in Diffusion Models

Diffusion models work by:

Image
↓
Add Noise
↓
Increase Entropy
↓
Random Distribution

Generation:

Random Distribution
↓
Remove Noise
↓
Decrease Entropy
↓
Image

The entire process can be viewed as moving between low and high entropy states.

---

Entropy in Physics

In thermodynamics:
Entropy measures disorder.

Low entropy:
Ice cube.
Highly structured.

---

High entropy:

Steam.
Highly disordered.

The second law of thermodynamics states:
Entropy tends to increase over time.

HERMES CAN NOW TEXT YOU ON IMESSAGE, EDIT YOUR IMAGES, AND RUN RESEARCH IN THE BACKGROUND WHILE YOU DO SOMETHING ELSE. IT'S FREE, OPEN SOURCE, AND RUNS ON YOUR OWN MACHINE.

nous research shipped v0.17.0 today "the reach release." 1,475 commits, 245 developers, 198,000 github stars. the whole point of this version: hermes stopped living in a terminal and moved into the apps you already use

the four things that actually matter:

→ iMessage support with one command hermes photon login. no mac relay, no jailbreak. your agent texts you in blue bubbles 
→ background subagents delegate_task(background=true). your main agent hands off research, builds, long jobs to a subagent and keeps working. nothing blocks 
→ image editing upload a logo, say "make this blue" or "remove the background," done. image-to-image, locally 
→ desktop command center rebindable shortcuts, live subagent watch windows, native notifications. it feels like a real app now, not a wrapper

it connects to 23 messaging platforms. telegram, whatsapp, signal, slack, imessage, teams, all of it. one agent core, same behavior everywhere

here's the part that makes this different from every cloud assistant: it runs locally, learns your style across every messenger you use, and the whole thing is MIT licensed. no subscription. no per-token bill. no data leaving your machine

a year ago "AI assistant" meant a chat tab you alt-tabbed into. now it texts you from the apps you already live in and does the work in the background while you sleep

the assistant didn't get smarter this week. it got closer

what would you actually delegate to an agent that texts you when it's done

Diffusion policy-gradient methods for RL optimize the ELBO variational surrogate instead of the true log-likelihood. This allows the ELBO to drift from the true objective during optimization, producing misaligned gradients and unstable training. 

DiPOD from UCB proposes a direct fix: interleave self-distillation with policy-improving gradient updates and augment each diffusion policy-gradient step with an on-policy ELBO regularizer. This keeps the surrogate tightly bound to the true log-likelihood throughout training, stabilizing optimization and achieving higher rewards in robotics and control tasks.

Diffusion Policy Optimization without Drifting Apart 
Paper: https://t.co/IMSyHUMeXz

I started working with generative AI about three years ago.
 Very quickly, fashion shows and Catwoman became my favorite themes.
Creating a character was very interesting back then, but also very laborious.
Today it's so much easier; it's accessible to everyone.
A tribute to my first favorite themes Grok Imagine 1.5 but with incredible quality and exemplary ease

The one big gap is that it is blind - it can't handle images at all.

If they fix that, then perhaps this could become the best available model in the world.
